annotations.label: Action (informal)

anchor.snippet: action is a scalar quantity that describes how the balance of kinetic versus potential energy of a physical system changes with trajectory

annotations.note: No notion of physical 'action' (kinetic minus potential energy along a trajectory) is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Action for single particle (uniform motion)

anchor.snippet: the action is the momentum of the particle times the distance it moves, added up along its path

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of momentum or particle action.

annotations.label: Action as functional

anchor.snippet: action is a mathematical functional which takes the trajectory

annotations.note: No action functional is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Action of a ball in gravity

anchor.snippet: For a trajectory of a ball moving in the air on Earth the action is defined between two points in time

annotations.note: This concrete physics example has no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Lagrangian (generalization of KE − PE)

anchor.snippet: The energy difference in the simple action definition, kinetic minus potential energy, is generalized and called the Lagrangian for more complex cases.

annotations.note: No Lagrangian-mechanics 'Lagrangian' in Mathlib; only ring/charpoly and graph 'Hamiltonian' hits exist.

annotations.label: Planck's quantum of action

anchor.snippet: The Planck constant , written as h , is the quantum (the minimal possible amount) of action.

annotations.note: The Planck constant is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reduced Planck constant ħ = h/2π

anchor.snippet: It is related to the quantum of angular momentum , ħ , by the relation ħ = ⁠ h / 2 π ⁠ .

annotations.note: ħ appears only in a unicode-linter file, not as a defined constant.

annotations.label: Action integral with Lagrangian

anchor.snippet: The action is typically represented as an integral over time, taken along the path of the system between the initial time and the final time

annotations.note: No action integral of a Lagrangian along a path is formalized.

annotations.label: Action functional 𝒮[q(t)]

anchor.snippet: The action 𝒮[ q ( t )] is defined as the integral of the Lagrangian L for an input evolution between the two times

annotations.note: No definition of 𝒮[q(t)] as a Lagrangian time-integral exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hamilton's principle

anchor.snippet: According to Hamilton's principle , the true evolution q true ( t ) is an evolution for which the action

annotations.note: Hamilton's principle of stationary action is not in Mathlib (only Hamiltonian graphs/charpoly).

annotations.label: Abbreviated action

anchor.snippet: The abbreviated action 𝒮 0 (sometimes written as W ) is defined as the integral of the generalized momenta

annotations.note: No abbreviated action functional in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Maupertuis's principle

anchor.snippet: According to Maupertuis's principle , the true path of the system is a path for which the abbreviated action is stationary

annotations.note: No occurrences of Maupertuis in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hamilton's characteristic function

anchor.snippet: the time-independent function W ( q 1 , q 2 ,…, q N ) is called Hamilton's characteristic function

annotations.note: Hamilton's characteristic function W is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Action of generalized coordinate J_k

anchor.snippet: A variable J k in the action-angle coordinates , called the "action" of the generalized coordinate q k , is defined by integrating a single generalized momentum around a closed path in phase space

annotations.note: Action-angle coordinates are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Action of relativistic point particle

anchor.snippet: the action of a point particle of mass m travelling a world line C parametrized by the proper time

annotations.note: No relativistic particle action, world line, or proper time in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Metric tensor signature (mostly plus)

anchor.snippet: where g μν is the metric tensor ≈ (− c 2 ,1,1,1) .

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Lorentzian metric tensor or signature conventions.

annotations.label: Stationary action principle

anchor.snippet: Classical mechanics postulates that the path actually followed by a physical system is that for which the action is minimized

annotations.note: Stationary/least-action principle is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Maupertuis's principle (least length)

anchor.snippet: Maupertuis's principle (named after Pierre Louis Maupertuis) states that the path followed by a physical system is the one of least length

annotations.note: Maupertuis's principle has no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Hamilton's principle (integral equation)

anchor.snippet: Hamilton's principle states that the differential equations of motion for any physical system can be re-formulated as an equivalent integral equation

annotations.note: No formulation of Hamilton's variational principle exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hamilton's principal function

anchor.snippet: Hamilton's principal function S = S ( q , t ; q 0 , t 0 ) is obtained from the action functional

annotations.note: Hamilton's principal function S(q,t;q0,t0) is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hamilton–Jacobi equation

anchor.snippet: The Hamilton's principal function satisfies the Hamilton–Jacobi equation, a formulation of classical mechanics .

annotations.note: The Hamilton–Jacobi PDE is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euler–Lagrange equations

anchor.snippet: the requirement that the action integral be stationary under small perturbations is equivalent to a set of differential equations

annotations.note: No Euler–Lagrange equations are formalized in Mathlib (no calculus-of-variations infrastructure).

annotations.label: Maxwell's equations from stationary action

anchor.snippet: Maxwell's equations can be derived as conditions of stationary action .

annotations.note: Mathlib does not contain Maxwell's equations or their variational derivation.

annotations.label: Einstein–Hilbert action

anchor.snippet: The Einstein equation utilizes the Einstein–Hilbert action as constrained by a variational principle .

annotations.note: The Einstein–Hilbert action is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Free-fall trajectory is a geodesic

anchor.snippet: For a free falling body, this trajectory is a geodesic .

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Riemannian/Lorentzian geodesic theory; existing 'geodesic' hits are graph-theoretic.

annotations.label: Noether's theorem

anchor.snippet: Noether's theorem , which states that to every continuous symmetry in a physical situation there corresponds a conservation law

annotations.note: Noether's theorem (symmetry → conservation law) is not in Mathlib; all 'Noether' hits concern Noetherian rings/modules/spaces.

annotations.label: Path integral (sum over paths)

anchor.section: Path integral formulation of quantum field theory
anchor.snippet: the behavior of the system depends on all permitted paths and the value of their action. The action corresponding to the various paths is used to calculate the path integral

annotations.note: Feynman path integrals are not formalized in Mathlib.
